title: Education Freedom Scholarships and Opportunity Act
short_title: Education Freedom Scholarships and Opportunity Act
plain_summary: This bill lets people and businesses get tax credits when they donate money to scholarship programs that help students pay for school or job training. The government will allow up to ten billion dollars in tax credits each year, with half going to education scholarships and half going to job training programs. Each state gets money based on how many students they have and how many poor families live there. The Department of Education will create a website to keep track of all the donations and tax credits.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Education Freedom Scholarships and Opportunity Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Purpose
sections.text: The goal of this law is to encourage individual people and businesses to donate money to scholarships for students. These scholarships come through approved organizations that give scholarships and approved job training organizations. Each state gets to choose which organizations are approved.

sections.title: Section 101 References to the Internal Revenue Code of 1986
sections.text: Unless stated otherwise, whenever this part of the law mentions changing or removing a section, it means a section from the Internal Revenue Code of 1986. This code is the main federal tax law.

sections.title: Section 102 Tax Credits for Contributions to Eligible Scholarship-Granting Organizations and Eligible Workforce Training Organizations
sections.text: This section creates new tax credits for people and businesses who donate to approved scholarship organizations and job training organizations. Individual people can get a tax credit equal to the amount they donate, but no more than ten percent of their yearly income. The donation must be cash given to an approved scholarship organization or job training organization. A qualified expense means money spent on elementary or secondary school education as the state recognizes it, career and technical education for students, or job training and apprenticeship programs including costs for certificates and credentials. An eligible scholarship-granting organization must be a tax-exempt charity that gives scholarships only to individual elementary and secondary students who live in the same state where the organization is recognized or are members of a federally recognized tribe. The state must report the organization to the Secretary of Education as eligible. The organization must spend at least ninety percent of donations on scholarships and must give scholarships to more than one student, more than one family, and students at different schools. An eligible workforce training organization must be a tax-exempt charity that provides job training, workforce development, or apprenticeship training to potential students. This includes organizations that give scholarships for certificates and credentials. The state must report the organization as eligible and it must meet the same spending and diversity requirements as scholarship organizations. These organizations can include community colleges, workforce training programs, organizations that provide career education or apprenticeships, and community groups that provide training leading to certification. A qualifying scholarship is money given by an eligible organization to a student for education or job training purposes. The word State includes all fifty states, Washington DC, Puerto Rico, American Samoa, Guam, Northern Mariana Islands, US Virgin Islands, and the Bureau of Indian Education. Scholarships given to students are not considered help to the school or training provider that serves the student. Scholarship money does not count as income for the student or parents for tax purposes or other federal programs. Nothing in this law allows federal control over private, religious, or home education providers. No state or entity can exclude, discriminate against, or disadvantage any education provider based on religious character or beliefs. No state can discourage using scholarships for private or faith-based education services. This law does not change state and local government authority to fund education. No tax credit can be more than the taxpayer's federal income tax for the year. Taxpayers cannot sell or transfer their tax credits. Federal, state, and local tax benefits combined cannot exceed the total donations made. If a taxpayer cannot use the full credit in one year due to low tax liability, they can carry it forward for up to five years. Taxpayers must choose to use this credit. The credit can be used when calculating alternative minimum tax. For businesses, the credit equals their donations but cannot exceed five percent of their taxable income for the year. The same rules that apply to individuals also apply to businesses. Businesses must also choose to use this credit.

sections.title: Section 201 Education Freedom Scholarships and Opportunity Act Web Portal and Administration
sections.text: The Secretary of Education, working with the Secretary of the Treasury and Secretary of Labor, must create and maintain a website that lists all approved scholarship and job training organizations. The website lets taxpayers donate to these organizations and immediately get pre-approval for their tax credit and a receipt for their taxes. The website provides information about tax benefits and lets states submit and update information about their programs and approved organizations. This information includes who can get scholarships, what education expenses are allowed, what types of education providers are allowed, how much money organizations can use for administration, and what percentage of donations organizations give out each year. Taxpayers can choose to donate directly to organizations instead of using the website, but they or the organization must apply for and get pre-approval for the tax credit from the Secretary of Education working with the Secretary of Treasury. There is a limit of ten billion dollars per year on donations that qualify for tax credits. Half of this amount, five billion dollars, goes to scholarship organizations and half goes to job training organizations. Each year, the Secretary of Education working with the Secretary of Labor divides up the money among participating states. For scholarship organizations, each state first gets the same amount as the donations made in their state the previous year. The remaining money is divided among states based on twenty percent going by the number of children ages five through seventeen in each state, and eighty percent going by the number of children ages five through seventeen from poor families in each state. For job training organizations, each state first gets the same amount as donations made to job training organizations in their state the previous year. The remaining money is divided based on a system that shows demand for apprenticeships and job training in each state. No state can get less than one half of one percent of the total money for a year. States can work together to manage their allocation if their approved organizations serve students from all partner states. By November first each year, the Secretary of Education announces how much money each state gets. By January first each year, each state must give the Secretary of Education a list of their approved organizations and confirm they have authority to submit this list. If a state does not provide a list and has no eligible organizations already operating there, their money goes to other states. After April first, money not claimed by states can be given to other states that have eligible organizations. After five years or when scholarship donations reach two and a half billion dollars in one year, whichever comes first, the Secretary of Education must create a new way to divide money among states. This new method will consider how many students get scholarships through state programs funded by tax-credited donations and the total amount of these scholarships compared to state and local education spending. The same definitions from the tax code section apply here. Congress approves spending whatever money is needed to run this program starting in fiscal year twenty twenty five and each year after.
